author: @renalxdo

text:
the problem with these algorithms is they get stuck in a loop and it keeps recommending the same type of music to you or keeping you in a bubble. Related songs to a song are different for each user so it ends up showing you more of the same (you can test this yourself, make another spotify account and check, this also applies to top songs from an artist and even spotify official playlists). At least I notice this problem with youtube music recommendations, maybe spotify is better with regards to this (I don't use spotify that much) but I still don't like it. 
Another problem besides getting stuck in a bubble and accidentally reinforcing certain cultures of music over others is that algorithms can be intentionally manipulated to devalue certain types of music/content and users won't notice. This gives a few tech company executives the power to shape public opinion, even to the point of changing results of elections for example, by subtly shifting what content is valued or devalued. (this also affects what content is created in the first place as creators make content that gets more views or money). This is more clearly a problem for writing/language content recommendations like twitter or tiktok style apps, but I think it could apply to music too, music is also speech and can also show people and/or normalize certain ideas.
title: Comment on: How Spotify AI-Driven Recommendations Work (WSJ)
author: @RemotHuman

text: I personally like Spotifys algorithm. It’s recommended so many good songs to me over the years. I’m sure it can be better, however it’s the best out there imo
title: Comment on: How Spotify AI-Driven Recommendations Work (WSJ)
author: @KnowDAOself

text: As seems to be the experience of many commentors here, algorithms/recommendations are becoming an increasingly crucial element of differentiation between Spotify & its competitors (Apple Music, YouTube Music, Amazon Music etc). Music streaming is a unique industry in that all the players have virtually all the music content available (unlike movie streaming like Netflix where they won't have all of the world's movies), and everyone is priced the same ($10.99/month in the US). This may be how Spotify can differentiate from the crowd, though only time will tell if its enough to help drive profitability in the future. I made a video on Spotify's persistent lack of profits and how they compare vs the other streaming platforms, may be interesting to you.
title: Comment on: How Spotify AI-Driven Recommendations Work (WSJ)

text:
This video had so much potential! 
Interesting topic and high production value.But it didn't really get into how it works, but instead spent 50% of time talking about an odd bias story that isn't really a big issue in playlisting songs.
I already know how the algorithm works but when I found this video, I was expecting them to give us really useful Info. But turns out 80% of this video was clickbait.
Now let me share some of my knowledge 
The algorithm will study your user habits (it will study the type of music you listen to, the artists you follow e.t.c). Once the algorithm has gotten sufficient data from you, it will now look for other Spotify users that have similar music taste to you. Any song they listen to, the algorithm recommends to you. It's as simple as that really. I wasted my time watching this lol

text:
I don't like Spotify because after they take their cut of my subscription fee, the remaining should be split between the artists I listen to and no others. This is important because, if, for example, I only enjoy traditional black Christian gospel music (eg Kirk Franklin), I don't really want to financially support people who  overtly worship Lucifer. 
Or if I don't want to support a major gospel artist one month, the money, after the Spotify cut, should be given to the small startup artist I wanted to support. 
Patrick Riddick might have 1000 loyal fans who play his music. If we pay $10 monthly and Spotify takes 30%, a small gospel artist like Patrick Riddick should walk away with $7 x 1,000 loyal fans that month. Instead, the bulk of that $7 is maldistributed to people like Beyonce, Taylor Swift, Ed Sheeran, and Drake.
The system is not only unfair to the artist, but it presents a moral conflict for conservative users. 😢

author: @rashikkenn8798

text:
I like the sound quality better with Apple Music, so nowadays that is my streaming service. I miss the quality of Spotify song recommendations, though.
What Apple Music consistently misses is the fact that I don’t like low-BPM music and that I don’t like wailing.
I’d say that about one in every 20 Spotify-recommendations was to my taste, and only about half of their recommendations were for songs that I profoundly hated. With Apple Music it’s more like one in 50 successful recommendations to nine out of ten total misses. Still—the audio quality of Apple Music is so much better that it’s still the superior service.
I wouldn’t mind paying for Spotify just to get their recommendations, but I can’t be bothered to manually sync my likes across subscriptions. What I’d really like would be an Apple TV-like interface for audio—where all your audio subscriptions were integrated. Apple Music for the sound quality, Spotify for the recommendations, and Sound Cloud for the absence of content moderation!

text:
日本の官公庁の公的発注工事の契約条件って一方通行。
発注側の恣意的な主観で注文通りかどうか確認するため、対象物を破壊して中身検査して復旧させて注文に適合させる過程を工事業者側の負担と責任でやりたい放題なんだよね。
しかも、専門知識ない担当者が仕様の作成も、公金で外注丸投げもよくあるある。
だから、使いこなせないこともある。総務省の情報システム18億円ムダになったニュースとかでも象徴的。
契約条件変更不可。
専門職に責任も負担も丸投げで縦割りの狭い範囲でしか仕事しないから。
日本のショボい医療とか警察や司法も似たようなことやって重い負担を負わせて、何も知らない権力側が授益だけ貪る仕組み蔓延してるよね。
バッテリーの異常とかストレージの異常とか、計測制御課金業者側の祖用がアレだと、格差拡大と怨念増長どんどん悪化するしかないだろうね。

text: Fav tidbits from this: if you hit a 20 popularity score, you start getting an algorithmic push on release radar. If you hit about a 30, you start getting it on discover weekly as well. To get a popularity for a 20 on average about 3800 streams. And that 3,800 will get you a release radar push. You needed 2,500 before. So you need about 50% More Streams to get the same popularity index as before now. It’s gotten harder to hit to increase popularity scores, which makes it harder to get on algorithmic playlist. Market saturation could affect this too. The most popular song is the more streams you’re gonna get from Radio. The more you market, the more they’re gonna give you. The more popular you are, the more you get for free from them. But makes sense, because more popularity, the more data they have and the more they can recommend your music. Streams are the only things that matter. Saves, playlist adds, release date, etc- don’t really matter. They just benefit because they lead to more streams. Streams are a side effect of all the other things. It takes about 13,000 streams to get a discover weekly push.

text: Thank you for this, Andrew. We have made a similar analysis with 175k  songs we're extending it to 1.5mio songs, but it just needs time. However, one of the problems we have faced with the analysis, that there's one major driver that explains (r2) 95% of the predictiable success of a song, and that's this factor: major label and/or it's subsidiaries. When removing that factor our R-squared is close to 65%. So, honestly, I'm surprised you come up with 92% using only a fraction of samples to test with. Anyway, wishing you great success improving your model, curious to see and hear more!

text: My formula for the popularity index, that I explained in a video of two years ago, is more precise. As I showed, the algorithm considers all streams in the last two months, and weigh them differently, according to how recent they are. First week count far more than the fourth. The PI moreover is not purely logarithmic, which can be seen by considering all data from a complete range of artists. Anyway, for all it matters, they have given a decent approximation.

text:
Fun fact about the "pay to play" feature.
Artists accept reduced royalties in order for their songs to be prioritised. Initially it makes sense to sign up because the increased streams makes up for the decreased royalties. Then most artists signs up.
Now each artist is accepting less money, but since most music is being prioritised already, each individual artist will have no more streaming than they started out with.
But if they pull out of the deal, they will lose most of their streaming.
So TLDR: Its a scam where you lose money if you take part in it, and you lose money if you don't.

text:
The problem in general is that people have become convinced that "Art" as a concept is only the finished product, divorced from the artistic process and the inevitable associations one can't help but express as a byproduct of that journey.

This is the extension of society's current aufomated exhaserbation of what I consider our greatest emotional imparative: the desire to avoid emotional effort.

Albums- even the BEST albums -require a commitment of time and consideration, if they're not also laden with weighty emotions and difficult concepts. 
Ain't nobody got time for that these days, even if they enjoy it thoroughly once they actually sit and listen. Its just not profitable to big business to search for the one, meaty album that folks will muster the wherewithal to engage with when they can just feed you something that's far less likely to make you disengage.

text: I agree, but there is one benefit to the algorithm playlists- discovery. Those algorithms are made by watching everything everyone listens to, and then making a good guess as to what I might like, given my listening habits and those who listen to similar works. The algorithm serves it's purpose like anything else, but yes- something is lost in the algorithm playlists. This is why I primarily use them when looking for new songs, and when I find one I listen to the whole album, potentially followed by the artist's most played songs. So while your frustration is justified, it is worth noting that these machine based tools are just that. Treating those playlists as the finished content as you say would be a mistake, but they have their place.

author: @wesleyyates5370

text: I used to program radio stations, and we used software to help us create the overall sound we were going for. For example on a Top 40/Pop station, songs were placed in categories and labeled based on type, tempo, feel, etc. We didn’t want to play too many slow songs in a row, for example, or too many rock leaning songs in a row. The software, using the information we gave it regarding the songs along with the rules we established, would generate a daily playlist/log, but we always had to look at the log and make modifications because there would be unscheduled songs, where the software simply couldn’t find a song without breaking the rules. Also, we would move songs around, substitute and delete songs based on other factors. I bet AI has gotten much better at this. Regarding pay for play without disclosure, it’s definitely illegal over public airwaves, but that probably doesn’t apply online. By the time I got into radio in the mid ‘90s, most of the wild pay for play was a thing of the past, but I remember hearing crazy stories of DJs and program directors being offered all sorts of things ❄️ 💃 to play songs on the air. Funny part is they could take these things and not play the songs; they simply “reported” that they played them when they sent in a weekly report to the trade magazines, which was used to calculate the weekly charts. This was called a “paper add.” Other stations would add the song and only play it in overnights. The rules were very strict by the time I got into radio and there had to be a paper trail for everything we got from record companies, which had to be used for promotion and given to the listeners. But, from what I heard, radio back in the day was absolutely insane.

text:
kay so i *do* organise my playlists and making them is kinda a hobby for me? i find them and have a collection stored in my yt library and i also make em for friends sometimes when they come to me with a really specific request they can’t find. but there’s also like different levels to my organisation?

level one: a bunch of songs i liked during a period of my life so i threw them in a playlist eg. “headache playlist” a bunch of songs i could still stand to listen to at a time when i was getting lots of headaches 
(good for shuffling)

level two: the songs just sound similar or have a similar vibe eg. “cottagecore” the songs had a cottagecore sound to them
(good for shuffling)

level three: the songs are grouped based on lyrics with similar meanings or that paint similar scenarios but may have wildly different sounds to em eg. “sshhh” a playlist about starcrossed lovers in a secret relationship 
(okay for shuffling but i try to organise these in a way that the transitions between genres and sounds is less egregious and flows better, can be slightly jarring shuffled)

level four: songs are grouped together based on both vibe *and* lyrics to fit into some kind of super specific feeling or scenario eg. “[u n t i t l e d]” a playlist for songs that give the vibe of a jaded college kid with shitty parents
(good for shuffling)

level five: songs grouped based on vibe and lyrics and then organised into a sprcific order to tell a story eg. “slipping away” a playlist that tells the story of a person falling in love with someone who isn’t right for them and how they start to fall apart and all the waves of emotions and stages of grief that come with the breakdown of their relationship and how it all builds up to their eventual breakup and then how they navigate the after that and the journey they take to heal and the joy of finally getting over that person 
(shuffling these ones is an offence punishable by death)

text:
This is a great way to describe how I’ve always felt about the pervasiveness of shuffle. I always listen to albums in order, and I think it’s insane when people shuffle albums. It feels disrespectful to the artist and less meaningful.

When I first switched from listening to my iPod to streaming services, I remember being so outraged that the free tier of spotify forces you to shuffle. It makes it completely useless. Playing an album in order is the most basic function of a streaming service. I know they’re purposely putting obstacles in front of it because they want you to pay, and it worked on me obviously. But it pisses me off that shuffling is now the default way for people to listen to music, and it seems like we haven’t questioned this shift.

text: I still store mp3s on my phone, and i have a playlist of my favorite band's music organized from softest to heaviest, which is something o don't think an algorithm could do properly. It could certainly try, as there are elements of music that people would generally agree contribute to a sense of "softness" or "hardness", but algorithms tend to look at elements individually, whereas the human mind looks at the elements in context with one another. And that context results in those interpretations being so subjective, that another person organizing the same list of songs in the same manner would end up putting them in a different order, and that person would feel certain that each song is getting progressively heavier.

text: Semi related I used one of Spotifys "made for you" playlist for the emotion lonely. It started fine with songs from my liked songs such as Lonely Day by System of a Down and A Desolation Song by Agalloch both of which are somber songs, but part way through Spotify added I'm on a boat by the lonely island which is upbeat and silly. The algorithm picked up the word lonely in the bands name but was well designed enough to determine that the song didn't fit the mood. Plus Spotifys algorithm is supposedly able to analyze things like tempo, key, energy, and loudness but by all these metric I'm on a boat doesn't match any other song on the playlist. These algorithms don't understand words, they create mathematical equations that associate numerical values to words so they can be understood by a machine that at it's core is just doing algebra in binary but those machines are incapable of understanding context, double meanings, or non standard English among other things. This algorithm couldn't figure out that in  the context of what I was asking if for the name of a band is irrelevant. This is why it bothers me that LLM's are being called AI. They aren't intelligent, they are complex word calculators with no understanding of what they output.
title: Comment on: Spotify Algorithm Sucks

text:
When I first started listening to music on vinyl, I noticed that when I listen to music on my phone, I don't really actively listen to it, it's more of a background track. With vinyl however you actively choose to listen to the music and the listening becomes an activity in itself, rather than something that accompanies an activity. It just feel a lot more special. (I hope this makes sense)

I'm not saying that listening to music in the background and not always focusing on it is inherently a bad thing, we just have to remind ourselfs sometimes to actually listen and not just... hear.

text: My main tip for people making the switch is first using a service to transfer your playlist (like Soundiiz), and afterwards making sure you just play your library on silent 24/7 for a few days to a week to train the algorithms. metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
Tip for anyone who struggles with Apple Music song suggestions not being the best;  There is a way to fix that in Apple Music. I had that issue initially but I figured out how to fix it: go to settings, enable “use listening history”, and then any song you like make sure to heart/favorite/like it and then as you continue to use it, the personalized playlists like “(your Apple Music name)’s station”, then continue to like songs etc. then if some songs repeat on that station tap the station icon and it refreshes. it’s starts working really well when you do this

Edit/Correction (5/25): it is “Use Listening history” not “use offline line history”

Adding to this (date 5/25):
Most people when they go to stations that suggest for you individual and/or recommendations playlist. Some of them update at different times / time intervals (which it will show you how long ago it was updated).

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
The library organization is what has me hooked on Apple Music. With Apple Music, my library is organized just like iTunes, Zune, Foobar2000, etc. It basically works like my local MP3 library did back in the day. It doesn't try to shuffle in random stuff that's not part of my library.

All the others I've tried (YT, Amazon, Spotify, and Tidal) are too focused on playlists and trying to slip in related songs where I don't want them slipped in. I just want to select an artist, and have it play the songs that are 1. only in my library, and 2. only by that artist. Kind of like making a mixed CD of all my favorite Atreyu songs or whatever. If I choose Atreyu, I just want it to play Atreyu.
title: Comment on: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music
author: @ElMartilloBlanco

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
I guess I am that one person who likes a very specific kind of music that only exists on YouTube and Youtube Music. Over the years I went from being a heavy Apple Music user to trying out Spotify and just never being satisfied with it over the years, to the day when my dad got YouTube Premium for the family, now suddenly I could listen to that subgenre and little creators that weren't on Apple Music no issues. As time went by I noticed myself opening YouTube Music more and more and using Apple Music less, I never made the concious switch, it just happened on it's own. 

In case people are wondering what music this is that's only on YT Music, doomer remixes of Eastern European artists, f.e. Hungarian or Slovak doomer/doomerified songs and small artists who don't have tracks yet on Spotify or Apple Music. I discovered and listened to a at least 3 artists before they got big. Also, concerts, sometimes I just want the acoustic version and the vibes from a concert and that's not on Apple Music or Spotify.
title: Comment on: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music
author: @robertrompf

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text: I've used Spotify before, but I'm currently using YouTube Music. I find that it has a larger music library—almost every song is available since it's integrated with YouTube. The recommendation system is also similar, so the suggested songs are usually excellent. I use budget earbuds, so 320kbps audio quality is more than sufficient for me. Plus you can switch between audio to video or vice versa which is nice feature to have.
title: Comment on: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music
author: @main-Bystander

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
I’ve used both Apple Music and Spotify and is sticking with Spotify for now. The one thing that is keeping me from using Apple Music is its lack of multi-language support to display artist names and song names.

I listen to English, Chinese, and Japanese songs, but for Chinese and some Japanese artists, I’m much more familiar with their artist names and song names in their native languages. But when I use Apple Music, because my preferred system language for my Apple devices is English, almost all of my Chinese songs and Japanese song names got translated to English. This issue is especially severe with Chinese artists and songs as I can hardly recognise their names. I wish Apple Music could just implement an option to display artists and song names in their native languages.
title: Comment on: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music
author: @ekersus8830

text:
What keeps me with Apple Music isn't the better audio quality or integration with Apple devices (I recently switched to Android), but it's the old iTunes DNA. I'm an album listener first and foremost and Spotify is just awful for anyone like me that likes to have an actual 'library' of music. 

When I add an artist or album to my library it's treated like an iTunes purchase and made accessible in one cohesive library. On Spotify, adding an artist acts as a shortcut to their artist page. No 'library' as such to speak of, instead funneling you into playlists or top songs whilst albums are hidden further down. I miss the social features of Spotify, sharing my stats and creating playlists with friends are two things I really miss but the pros far outweigh the cons for me 😊

The whole “Mastered for iTunes” (and its new name under Apple Music) creates a different starting point that sounds “different” to a CD.  CDs are uncompressed audio and if you rip it to a WAV, it still doesn’t sound as good on any set of AirPods or AirPods Pro.  There are just details on the new mix you never heard on CD.

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
I switched to apple music almost a year ago now after how bad the wrap was last year, it wasn't the main thing because there were a lot of things that were pushing me to get off spotify. One of the things was that I am a picky listener, meaning I listen to a lot of the same songs over and over again along with the same bands, this is probably what caused spotify to almost never show me anything new to listen to but that's not an excuse when I was able to go apple music and find new music from both new and old bands. I also hated how the shuffle on spotify works and honestly surprised you didn't go into it in this video, it was geniuelly so bad for me because I started to not listen to certain tracks that I loved because spotify would always front load them in the shuffle. Apple music doesn't this issue as much, it's still there but at least It picks songs that are deep into my playlists that I haven't hear in a while for some shuffles.
I could also care less for the AI stuff that Spotify has been doing as of late, it doesn't improve the experience and makes it more feel more of a gimmicky shuffle. There was a time that I geniuelly used Spotify's podcast catalog because when I worked and was allowed to wear earbuds I would get tired of listening to music(thanks to spotify's abhorrent shuffle) and listen to podcasts but only at work. 
I also don't like that it's turn into more of a 'social' app, with additions like short format videos that add nothing to the experience other than cluttering up the homepage. Apple music is just about music and nothing else, it's homepage is simple, but at least I'm not apposesed to exploring it for other stations or possible new albums to listen to. Apple music may look like it's iTunes predasser but if it ain't broke, don't fix it.

Overall, spotify for me in the end just wasn't working out and I found that Apple music just suited my needs more, and with all my apple devices I have it just works(as much as I hate saying that line). I don't think I'll go back to spotify anytime soon, unless they do some drastic changes to their business model that makes them a overall better option than Apple music.
title: Comment on: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
Having used Spotify, Apple Music, and now YouTube Music, over the past 10 years, here's my take.

Spotify had the best music discovery feature for me, but could not justify spending the money on it, as the others were included in other bundles.
Apple Music was the easiest to kind of just start and go without thinking too much, with good sound quality and ability to have downloaded music on the Apple Watch as well (useful when going for a run if you don't use watch cellular). I used it because it was included in Apple One which made financial sense for me at that time.
YouTube Music has the best "create station" feature for me, quality is on par with Apple, the library is by far the biggest IMO, and already knows so much about me having used YouTube for so many years. Not as easy to start random playlists (interface makes it a bit more confusing as to what is in each of them or what is new), does not remember the previously played one if the app gets killed in the background, and no Apple Watch support (only as a remote, not player). But included in my YouTube Premium which is a no brainer anyway for someone spending time on YouTube, so definitely worth it overall.

most headphones and anything bluetooth enabled doesn’t even support lossless audio. you’ll only get lossless audio on high end wired studio headphones so the fact that it sounds good and stuff is just pure delusion

oh and decent headphones (like airpods pro, airpods max, sony xm5) already have spatial processing and they give you surround sound in the form of spatial mode which is pretty much dolby atmos

When I discovered Spotify, it was life changing for me. Had it for 2-3 years and absolutely loved it.

I personally couldn’t give a shit about being “social” with my music. It’s just my daily therapy and Apple gives it to me in amazing lossless quality and hi-res lossless in some tracks. 

Apple killed Spotify with lossless! For real…

text:
Whilst you touched on the two apps being able to airplay to different devices, that's great it you have a totally Apple ecosystem. For those of us who have Amazon Echo devices (aka Alexa), Spotify wins out. Echo's can natively play music from Apple Music via voice commands, but only Spotify gives you direct hands-on control via the app.

Yes, you can connect an Apple device to an Echo via Bluetooth, but it's a limited workaround. For instance, I have a stereo pair of Echo's in my lounge, and groups set up for other Echo's in the house. You can only connect to one at a time via Bluetooth.

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
I've been using Spotify since 2016, I've recently tried both Amazon music and YouTube music too. I find I like YouTube music the most for that reason, but I HATE how they integrate playlists into your normal YouTube playlists AND they don't allow you to sort playlists into folders like Spotify, I also love how easy it is to find new music on Spotify, but YouTube music isn't as effortless to do so. I also hate how YouTube music doesn't show your recently listened to playlists at the top when you open it and with the lack of folders can make it annoying to go find the playlists you've been listening to.

I have a TON of playlists on Spotify (one for each year that I make every January where I add songs / albums I like and find during that year, then playlists for each genre, moods / themes, seasons, and collab playlists with my GF and friends, etc.) and that on top of YouTube music not having folders AND having those playlists visible on normal YouTube makes it feel so frustrating and cluttered to use after I transferred all my playlists over. The UI in those respects make me stop using it and go back to Spotify after a short bit, It just doesn't work with how I like to use and organize playlists and I don't like it cluttering up my normal YouTube playlists so when I try to save normal YouTube videos to a playlist I have to sort through ALL these music playlists.

My only big gripe with Spotify is the lack of CD or HD quality audio files and the shuffle algorithm sucks sometimes (I've already tried all settings to fix it, the only real fix is clearing my cache to temporarily fix it). Most of the time it's perfectly fine though and I find it's the easiest platform to search / delve down rabbit holes, however YouTube music now has a near identical layout for doing so where the artists page has the "fans also like" and the "featured on" sections and you can find playlists made by other people, but there is no friends lists so you can see what your friends listen to and there's no collaborative playlists or Jam sessions available to my knowledge? Spotify makes it much easier to explore and share music in those ways. I also prefer how Spotify integrates into android auto more.

I want to use YouTube music especially since I already pay for YouTube premium, but those gripes with the playlists and collaborative / friend stuff just ruins it. Yeah, it has more niche music which I like but those other differences just make it more of an inconvenience and it doesn't offer much else that Spotify doesn't offer. If they changed the things I have issues with I would switch in a heartbeat though.

Amazon music's HD audio was, listened with my soundcard in my PC with my IEMs and with a small external DAC for my phone and it sounded great, but the UI is just horrible. so horrible. and it suffers from the same complaints as YouTube music but with even MORE quirks and things I didn't like (plus a smaller music pool AND you still have to pay for it on top of your Amazon Prime which is just stupid). Listening to HD audio is nice, but its not worth the hassle for me and going with the smaller music pool. When I'm driving or at work and I want to listen to my main playlists, artists, or albums I just prefer the convenience of Spotify. It's easy and effortless to use / integrates well with every device (and the PC application for Spotify is great).

I also love how Spotify lets you organize / filter and sort your library on PC and on the phone, with the folders and the quick pick filter options (that also retains your filter options between sessions) makes it so easy to filter by the albums, artists, playlists (made by only you, Spotify, or both) for you want each session, and it filters it instantly. on YouTube music when you select a filter option it takes a few seconds to reload your library which is just a minor nitpick / frustration for day to day use AND YouTube music doesn't save your filter options between sessions. On Spotify I always keep it filtered and sorted to show MY playlists and in recently played order and I never have to reset that filter option, but YouTube music I do have to reset it each time I open the app and go to my library (and trying to find a playlist in your library with Android Auto is annoying).

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
3:46 One niche thing to overlook... you technically don't "need" specific hardware for Atmos. That's just a bunch of marketing BS to sell head tracking devices. And personally, I hate head tracking. If you have headphones, you can have Atmos, even without head tracking. I would argue that the better selling point for Dolby Atmos music isn't the headphone experience, but rather a more dynamic version of the song in general, since it doesn't get squashed to maximum loudness like most stereo songs released today. It's not for everyone, but you can listen to Atmos music on just about anything if you enable it to do so. The quality of your experience will depend on your hardware and how well the song was mixed. The same concept applies for stereo as well. A badly mixed stereo song will be obvious, but it is an even more obvious with Atmos when there is a bad mix. And there are LOADS of bad mixes out there right now. 

Also, if you have a Mac or AppleTV,  and do have the appropriate hardware to play on an Atmos speaker system, as far as I know, Apple Music is the only place you can stream 7.1.4 Music directly to your speakers.
title: Comment on: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music
author: @alex_ayers

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text:
I love Apple Music’s UI and audio quality, but Spotify’s algorithm is just so much better. My frustration with Apple Music was that whenever I played a song, I usually didn’t like the next set of recommendations. I used it for more than a year, and most of the time I’d either just play from my own playlists or turn the music off altogether.

Now I’m on Spotify (I got 3 months free from a coupon), and it instantly picked up on my preferences. I’m loving it. There’s no rush to skip songs anymore. I’m always excited for the next track, I’ve even created blends, and overall I’m really enjoying the experience.

Spotify has a custom Equalizer function. You have to address this major difference. When discussing a music listening app it blows my mind that iTunes has no custom EQ function. I used iTunes all through the early years (2005-2015ish) and slowly watched its functionality fail to the point that I use Spotify exclusively now (some Soundcloud on occasion). 

Also, the method for queuing songs in Spotify is better than Apple Music.

I need a lot of changes to functionality, recommended songs, and EQ on Apple Music to go back.

metadata.video_title: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music

text: Been using both Apple Music and Spotify for the last 3 years. Spotify for car and gym, Apple music for intentional music listening. Lossless and Spatial audio is very valuable to me. I also listen to a fair bit of classical music and the Apple music classical app is the best music app I have ever used. I would use apple music for everything but I value the social aspect of Spotify too much. The music discovery is also significantly better than apple music but I'm only listening to songs I love on apple music anyway. I have both a Samsung and an iPhone. Ironically, I never use apple music on the iPhone since it doesn't support lossless audio via Bluetooth to my Sony WH-1000XM6's. I've tried Tidal for 6 months and it was great but annoying that it had separate tracks for compressed, lossless and spatial audio versions of a song and are hard to find all versions.
title: Comment on: I Switched From Spotify To Apple Music
author: @Zazi_Ndebele

metadata.video_title: Spotify VS Apple Music Who WINS

text:
Go Apple Music if:

	•	You care about sound quality and want lossless / high resolution.
	•	You have good listening gear (good headphones, DACs, etc).
	•	You’re invested in the Apple ecosystem (macOS, iOS, HomePods).
	•	Immersive audio by(Dolby Atmos / Spatial Audio) matters to you.
———

 Stick with (or choose) Spotify if:

	•	You mostly listen casually (on phone, car speakers, Bluetooth) so differences are hard to notice.
	•	Discovery, social features, podcasts are high on your list.
	•	Cross-platform flexibility matters (non-Apple hardware, etc).

text:
First of all thanks for the info, it was very enlightening.
(For my taste there was way too much cut in the video and that was annoying to me. Perhaps I am  oldscool and younger minds find these things appealing.)

But, let me give you some other perspective: The biggest problem with CONTENT CREATION that you HAVE TO do it week by week (to earn money). There is no real quality in these products. Just some kind of copy paste. Looking for trends, adjusting data, again searching for new things that maybe interested by people, develop some, and release new content. Thats sick. It will NEVER give you real things. This is just the way to the point of burnout and deep depression. My advice: forget about you HAVE TO do it. Do it when it is ready. Quality over quantity.
Cheers and have a nice day!
title: Comment on: How to BLOW UP on Discover Weekly
author: @eftomi
